title: Term Insurance vs Endowment Plan 2026 — Which Is Better?
meta_description: Complete comparison of term insurance vs endowment plans: coverage, returns, premiums, tax benefits, and who should buy what. 2026 guide with real numbers.

hero.one_liner: Term insurance vs endowment plan — the single most important insurance decision most Indians get wrong. Here's what each actually offers, real-number comparisons, and who should buy which one.
hero.updated_date: Last reviewed: April 2026 • Sources: IRDAI, LIC, Licensed Agents

sections.heading: What Are These Two Plans?
content: Term Insurance is pure life insurance. You pay a small premium, and if you die during the policy term, your family receives the sum assured (say ₹1 crore).
If you survive the term, you get nothing back — no money returned. It's an insurance product, not an investment.
Endowment Plan is insurance combined with investment. You pay much higher premiums, and get either (a) the sum assured if you die, OR (b) the sum assured + bonuses back on maturity if you survive.
Sounds like the best of both worlds — except the returns are poor and the coverage is inadequate.

The fundamental question: should insurance and investment be separate, or combined? Financial experts almost universally recommend separating them — buy term insurance for protection + mutual funds for investment. Together, these deliver far better outcomes than any endowment plan.

Yet endowment plans dominate Indian life insurance sales because agents earn 15-35% commission on endowment premiums vs just 2-5% on term insurance. This is why your relative, neighbor, or LIC agent keeps pushing you to buy 'money back policies' and 'traditional plans.'

sections.heading: The Truth Most Agents Won't Tell You
sections.content: LIC agents earn 15-35% commission on endowment plans but only 2-5% on term insurance. This is why every agent and relative pushes you toward endowment — not because it's better for you.

sections.heading: Term vs Endowment — Head to Head

sections.text: Same ₹50,000 premium gets you ₹5 CRORE term cover or ₹6 LAKH endowment cover. That's 80x more protection for the same money.

sections.caption: Same ₹50,000 premium — term gives ₹5 crore cover, endowment gives ₹6 lakh
sections.description: This is why every financial advisor recommends term for protection. Your family needs ₹1-2 crore cover to replace your income. Endowment plans simply can't afford to give you that much cover because most of your premium goes into the 'investment' portion.

sections.heading: Why Term Wins — Real Numbers
content: Let's do actual math. A 30-year-old earning ₹10 lakh/year needs ₹1-1.5 crore life cover (10-15x income). Here are two approaches:
Strategy A: Term + Mutual Fund (Recommended) — Buy ₹1 crore term insurance for ₹10,000/year. Invest remaining ₹40,000/year in an equity mutual fund SIP.
Over 25 years, the term cost is ₹2.5 lakh. The ₹40,000/year mutual fund (at 12% CAGR) grows to ₹60 lakh.
Plus you had ₹1 crore cover for 25 years. Total outcome: ₹60 lakh + protection.
Strategy B: Endowment (What agents sell) — Buy ₹10 lakh endowment for ₹50,000/year. No mutual fund since endowment takes up budget.
Over 25 years, you pay ₹12.5 lakh total premiums. Maturity amount ~₹20-25 lakh (4-5% IRR).
Death cover only ₹10 lakh throughout. Total outcome: ₹20 lakh + inadequate protection.
The difference is massive: Strategy A delivers 3x more wealth AND 10x more insurance cover. Yet endowment outsells term 4:1 in India because of commission structures.

sections.caption: Term + Mutual Fund gives 2.5x more wealth than Endowment
sections.description: Both strategies use the same ₹50,000/year budget. The difference is where the money goes — equity markets grow at 12% historically, while endowment plans return only 4-6%.

sections.heading: When Endowment Might Make Sense
content: Endowment plans aren't completely useless. There are specific niche cases where they could work:
1. You have ZERO discipline for investing — If you know you'll blow bonus money on spending, a forced 20-year endowment commitment creates artificial discipline.
But even then, SIP auto-debit is a better solution.
2. You want zero-risk guaranteed returns — Endowment maturity amounts are (partially) guaranteed.
For someone who panics during market crashes, this psychological safety may be worth the lower returns. But PPF offers 7.1% with similar safety — better than endowment's 4-6%.
3. You're over 50 and want post-retirement income — Some endowment/whole life plans offer monthly pension after 60.
Useful if you have no other retirement corpus. But SWP from mutual funds usually beats this.
4. Agent is family member whose relationship matters — Sometimes the emotional cost of refusing family is real.
Buy a small endowment (₹2-3L annual premium), treat it as sunk cost, and buy separate term + MF for your actual needs.
For 95% of people: buy TERM insurance + invest separately in mutual funds. Avoid endowment plans entirely.

sections.heading: For 95% of People
sections.content: Buy Term insurance + invest separately in mutual funds. This "BTID" strategy (Buy Term, Invest the Difference) delivers 2-3x more wealth AND better protection than any endowment plan.

sections.heading: How to Calculate Your Life Cover Need
content: Your life insurance need depends on your family's dependency on your income. Three methods to calculate:
1. Human Life Value (HLV) Method — Most accurate.

Formula: Annual income × (Years to retirement) + Major liabilities (home loan, education costs) - Existing savings. For a 30-year-old earning ₹10L/year with 30 years to retirement, no debt, HLV ≈ ₹3 crore (conservative estimate assuming 6% discount rate).
2. Income Multiplier (Simpler) — Your cover should be 15-20x your annual income.
Earning ₹10L? Cover ₹1.5-2 crore.
This is easier to calculate and usually sufficient.
3. Expense Replacement — Your cover should support your family's expenses until kids become self-sufficient.
Monthly family expenses × 12 × 25 years. Family spending ₹50K/month?
Need ₹1.5 crore cover (50K × 12 × 25).

Whichever method you use, remember: cover below ₹1 crore is usually inadequate in Indian urban context. And always review cover every 5 years — as family grows and liabilities change, your cover needs change too.

sections.heading: Top Term Insurance Plans in India 2026
How to Buy Term Insurance — Step by Step

steps.title: Decide your cover amount
steps.description: Use the 15-20x income rule or HLV calculation. Minimum ₹1 crore for most urban salaried. Consider inflation — ₹1 crore today = ₹50 lakh in 15 years.

steps.title: Choose policy term
steps.description: Cover should extend till retirement (age 60-65) or until your youngest child is self-sufficient. Most people choose 30-40 year terms starting at age 25-30.

steps.title: Compare plans on aggregator sites
steps.description: Visit PolicyBazaar, Ditto, or Turtlemint. Compare: premium, claim settlement ratio (prefer >97%), solvency ratio, riders offered. IRDAI publishes annual claim data — check recent year.

steps.title: Add essential riders
steps.description: Critical Illness rider (covers cancer, heart attack etc.) — ₹500-1,000 extra premium. Accidental Death Benefit — doubles payout if death is accidental. Waiver of Premium — premiums waived if you become disabled.

steps.title: Complete medical tests
steps.description: Required for covers above ₹50 lakh. Blood test, ECG, urine test at insurer-approved lab (free for you). Be 100% truthful about smoking, drinking, medical history — lying here voids the policy entirely.

steps.title: Pay first premium + document signing
steps.description: Online signing via Aadhaar OTP. Payment via UPI/card/netbanking. Policy document sent via email + physical copy in 7-14 days.

steps.title: Inform family about the policy
steps.description: Share policy number, insurer contact, premium payment date, claim process with your spouse/parents. Store documents in DigiLocker and tell family where to find them. Keep a nominee carefully chosen.

steps.title: Renew premium every year
steps.description: Set up auto-debit for premium payments. Missing even one premium after grace period (30 days) lapses the policy entirely — no partial benefits. Some insurers offer one-time revival within 5 years with medical re-check.

sections.heading: Tax Benefits Comparison
content: Both term and endowment plans offer tax benefits, but the context differs:
Section 80C (Old Tax Regime Only) — Premiums paid for life insurance are tax-deductible up to ₹1.5 lakh/year. Applies to both term and endowment.
But most people already hit this ₹1.5L limit via EPF + PPF — so insurance premiums may not give additional tax benefit.
Section 10(10D) — Tax-Free Maturity — If annual premium is less than 10% of sum assured, maturity amount is tax-free. Term insurance: payout to family is always tax-free (Section 10(10D)).
Endowment: Maturity tax-free IF premium < 10% of sum assured, which is usually the case. But for high-premium policies (like ULIP-style endowments), maturity may be taxed.
New Tax Regime (2024 onwards) — Under the new regime (default from FY 2024-25), Section 80C deduction is NOT available. So you don't get premium deduction.
However, Section 10(10D) tax-free maturity still applies.
Bottom line: Don't buy insurance just for tax savings. Term insurance's real value is cheap protection.
If you're buying endowment for tax savings alone, PPF gives better tax benefits and guaranteed 7.1% returns.

sections.caption: The single decision that could save you ₹30-50 lakh over 30 years
sections.description: This approach, popularly known as 'BTID' (Buy Term, Invest the Difference), is recommended by every credible financial advisor. Simple, transparent, flexible — and mathematically optimal.

sections.heading: Editorial Note
content: This comparison was written by the KnowledgeKendra editorial team and last fact-checked in April 2026. Claim settlement ratios and premium ranges are sourced from IRDAI's public annual report for FY 2024-25.
Premiums vary by age, health condition, smoker status, and policy term — numbers shown are indicative for a 30-year-old non-smoker male.

This is educational content, not personalized insurance advice. Before buying any insurance, compare at least 3-4 insurers, read the policy wording carefully, and consult a licensed insurance agent if needed.
Disclosure: KnowledgeKendra has no commercial relationship with any insurer or aggregator. Plan rankings are based on publicly available claim ratios from IRDAI as of March 2026.
Insurer names are mentioned for comparison purposes only.

faqs.question: Should I surrender my existing endowment plan?
faqs.answer: It depends on where you are in the policy. If you're in the first 3 years, surrendering results in huge losses (you may get back only 30-50% of premiums paid). If you've paid for 5+ years, calculate: (Maturity you'd get from continuing) vs (Surrender value now + switching to term+MF). Use an online endowment plan calculator. For most people with 10+ years remaining, switching to term+MF is still worth the loss. Consult a fee-only financial planner before deciding — stakes are high.

faqs.question: What if I already have LIC endowment policies?
faqs.answer: Common situation. Don't panic. Continue paying existing policies to maturity (surrender loss is usually greater than future underperformance). But immediately: (1) Buy a ₹1 crore term plan on top for adequate cover. (2) Start an SIP in mutual funds for wealth building. (3) Don't buy any NEW endowment/money-back/ULIP plans. Your existing endowments will still give you some maturity amount — treat it as a conservative part of your portfolio.

faqs.question: Is term insurance enough? Don't I need something I can get back?
faqs.answer: This is the most common psychological block. Think about it: car insurance, health insurance, home insurance — you don't 'get back' premiums if nothing happens. Yet nobody refuses to buy these. Life insurance works the same way. Term insurance provides the cover your family desperately needs if something happens to you. If nothing happens, you've paid a tiny price (₹10K/year) for 20+ years of financial security. The 'something I can get back' instinct is exactly how insurance companies profit — by selling you poor returns disguised as both insurance and investment.

faqs.question: What about ULIP plans — are they better than endowment?
faqs.answer: ULIPs (Unit Linked Insurance Plans) combine insurance with market-linked investment. They're marginally better than traditional endowment plans because part of your money goes into equity markets. But ULIPs still charge 2-4% in various fees annually (mortality charges, fund management, administration), significantly dragging down returns. Pure equity mutual funds charge 0.5-1.5%. So ULIP returns are 1-2% lower than equivalent mutual fund returns over 20 years — that's ₹20-30 lakh lost on a ₹50K/year investment. Stick with term + mutual funds.

faqs.question: At what age should I buy term insurance?
faqs.answer: Ideally between ages 25-35. Younger = lower premiums (locked in for entire term). Earlier you buy, cheaper it stays. Waiting until 40 increases premiums by 50-80%. If you're 35+, don't delay further — buy now. If you're 45+, you can still buy but premiums will be significantly higher (₹25,000-40,000/year for ₹1 crore cover).

faqs.question: Which is the best term insurance plan?
faqs.answer: No 'best' plan exists for everyone. Look for: (1) Claim Settlement Ratio above 97% — LIC, HDFC Life, Max Life are strong. (2) Solvency Ratio above 1.5 — indicates financial health. (3) Simple features (avoid 'return of premium' variants — they defeat the purpose). (4) Online plans generally cheaper than offline. Use PolicyBazaar or Ditto to compare 4-5 insurers side by side. Pick the one with best claim ratio at reasonable premium.

faqs.question: What does 'claim settlement ratio' mean?
faqs.answer: Percentage of claims an insurer paid out vs total claims filed in a year. A 97% CSR means 97 out of 100 valid claims were paid. IRDAI publishes this annually. Higher = better. Most top insurers are 97-99%. Avoid insurers below 95% — your family might face rejection. Check the LATEST year's CSR, not multi-year averages (which can hide recent worsening). CSR doesn't guarantee your claim will be paid, but high ratio reduces risk significantly.

faqs.question: What's the difference between term plan and whole life insurance?
faqs.answer: Term insurance covers you for a specific period (20-40 years). Whole life covers you until age 99-100. Term is MUCH cheaper (because most people outlive term period, insurer rarely pays out). Whole life always pays out eventually, so premiums are 5-10x higher. For 95% of people, term is better — you need protection mainly during earning years (when family depends on you). After retirement, you have assets, not income dependency. Buy term.

faqs.question: Should I buy term online or through an agent?
faqs.answer: Online is usually better — cheaper premiums (5-15% lower), no intermediary, faster processing. Most top insurers have dedicated online platforms. Agents add value only if: (1) You have complex medical history needing explanation. (2) You need hand-holding for claim process later. (3) You're buying high-value cover (₹5+ crore) where case-by-case underwriting matters. For simple ₹1-2 crore plans, online wins.

faqs.question: What if the insurance company shuts down?
faqs.answer: IRDAI mandates strict solvency requirements (Solvency Ratio > 1.5). If an insurer struggles, IRDAI arranges a merger with another insurer — your policy continues uninterrupted. No Indian life insurer has ever shut down leaving policyholders stranded (unlike banks like Yes Bank). Even private insurers are extremely safe because IRDAI's oversight is stringent. That said, prefer insurers with track record of 10+ years and solid solvency ratios.

faqs.question: Can I reduce term insurance premium later?
faqs.answer: No, premium is locked at the time of buying. You pay the same amount for the entire term (20-40 years). This is actually the advantage of buying young — you lock in cheap premiums forever. A 25-year-old paying ₹8,000/year will pay ₹8,000 even at age 55 (when same cover would cost ₹30,000 to a new 55-year-old buyer). If your needs change, you can reduce SUM ASSURED (some insurers allow this) — but premium for current term stays fixed.

faqs.question: What happens if I can't pay premium one year?
faqs.answer: There's a 30-day grace period after premium due date. Within this time, pay without penalty. After 30 days, policy lapses — coverage stops, you lose protection. Most insurers offer revival option within 2-5 years: pay pending premiums + interest + medical re-check. If revival not done, policy is permanently cancelled. Solution: set up auto-debit for term insurance — missing a ₹10K/year premium costs your family ₹1 crore if something happens. Never let term lapse over a small premium.
